question: Explain the molecular mechanism whereby cytotoxic T-lymphocyte–associated protein 4 (CTLA-4) blockade augments anti-tumor immunity and identify the intracellular signals that become dysregulated during acquired resistance.
answer:
CTLA-4 is a transmembrane glycoprotein expressed on activated T-cells and regulatory T-cells (Tregs); it functions as a high-affinity, non-signaling decoy receptor that competes with CD28 for binding to B7-1 (CD80) and B7-2 (CD86) on antigen-presenting cells.  Engagement of CTLA-4 recruits the tyrosine phosphatases SHP-2 and PP2A to its cytoplasmic tail, leading to de-phosphorylation of the CD3ζ chain and MAPK pathway suppression, thereby limiting T-cell receptor (TCR) signaling amplitude and proliferation.  Pharmacologic blockade—e.g., ipilimumab (human IgG1 anti-CTLA-4)—prevents this inhibitory signal, sustaining IL-2 production and clonal expansion of tumor-specific CD8+ effector T-cells.
Acquired resistance mechanisms include (i) upregulation of alternative immune checkpoints (TIM-3, LAG-3, and VISTA) that re-impose anergy; (ii) epigenetic silencing of the IFN-γ pathway (loss of JAK1/2 or STAT1 phosphorylation), which reduces MHC-I expression and tumor-cell recognition; and (iii) infiltration of CTLA-4-resistant Treg subsets expressing OX40 and GITR that secrete IL-10 and adenosine, establishing a suppressive tumor microenvironment.
Clinically, patients receiving combination ipilimumab+ nivolumab achieve 10-year overall survival (OS) rates of 26 % in metastatic melanoma versus ~16 % with nivolumab alone, but resistance correlates with somatic loss-of-function mutations in JAK1 or B2M detected via liquid biopsy (CheckMate 067 and 214 extended follow-up, NEJM 2025).
Summary:  CTLA-4 blockade rescues TCR signaling by removing SHP-2/PP2A-mediated de-phosphorylation; resistance emerges via checkpoint redundancy, IFN-γ signaling loss, and Treg-mediated metabolic suppression.
